summary: Drew DeVault reflects on his first months living in Amsterdam after relocating from the United States in July 2021. He describes finding housing through the hacker community, building a social network via the Techinc hacker space, and falling in love with Amsterdam's cycling infrastructure and public transit. He praises the city's parks, walkable neighborhoods, accessible grocery stores, and street cleanliness, while noting that learning Dutch has been more challenging than expected. Overall, he expresses deep satisfaction with his decision to move abroad.
key_points: Amsterdam's cycling infrastructure, with 38% of trips by bike and dedicated bike-only roads, is transformatively better than American cities
The Dutch public transit system with unified NFC payment, frequent service, and excellent inter-city trains makes Amtrak look embarrassing by comparison
Walkable neighborhoods with multiple grocery stores within walking distance led to an unexpectedly improved diet
The hacker community provided both housing and a social network, making the transition far smoother than feared
Learning Dutch proved surprisingly difficult beyond the basics despite his existing background in languages
money_quotes: In Philadelphia, travelling by bicycle is signing up for a death wish.
The whole country is very flat, too, though Dutch people will be quick to tell you about The Hill in their neighborhood, which is always no more than 2 meters tall.
I can show up at any major station without a plan and there's probably a train heading to where I want to go in less than 10 minutes. Compared to Amtrak, it's simply mind boggling.
Thanks to the ease of accessing good (and cheap) produce and other ingredients, my diet has improved quite a bit — something I didn't expect when I moved here.
I can't get everything I want, though: finding genuinely spicy chili peppers is a challenge.
Every time I've paused to reflect on my relocation abroad, I've come away smiling.
key_insight: Relocating from the US to the Netherlands revealed how profoundly urban design choices around cycling, transit, and walkability shape daily quality of life in ways Americans rarely experience.
